retrieved_docs.title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The preop evaluation generally includes a history and physical as well as baseline electrolytes, CBC, PSA, a post-void residual determination, and urinalysis, which are reasonable preoperative tests prior to urologic surgery. Coagulation studies are no longer considered necessary unless there is a history of unexplained or unusual bleeding. Renal imaging studies and urodynamics are also unnecessary in most cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Surgical Oncology -- Radical Prostatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Individual patient issues include activity level, age, continence, comorbidities, performance status, presurgical erectile function, the necessity of lymphadenectomy, and whether a nerve-sparing technique is utilized. A bilateral nerve-sparing approach is recommended whenever it does not compromise the complete removal of the malignancy. MRI imaging is beneficial in making these determinations. [265]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Surgical Oncology -- Complications of Radical Prostatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of radical prostatectomy include erectile dysfunction, especially if no nerve-sparing surgery was performed; urinary incontinence, especially stress incontinence that is reported in 52% of patients initially; urethral strictures, affecting 8% to 11% of patients; and an increased risk of inguinal hernias, occurring in 6% to 8% of cases. Overall mortality rates are less than 1% in most series. Rates for erectile dysfunction vary greatly depending on preoperative potency and age, the type of surgery performed (nerve-sparing or not), and the use of penile rehabilitation techniques. [278] [279]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When considering treatment, large randomized controlled trials have demonstrated equivalent overall and disease-specific mortality of active surveillance, radical radiotherapy, and radical surgery at 10 years of follow-up. Thus, it is important to assess each patient individually to establish his co-morbidities and priorities to optimize treatment and minimize side effects (for example, not offering radiotherapy to a patient with pre-existing colitis).

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Prognosis -- Do We Absolutely, Positively Need to Have a Positive Prostate Tissue Biopsy to Treat Prostate Cancer?
retrieved_docs.content: Although a positive tissue biopsy is always preferred before treatment, situations arise where it may not be practical or obtainable. Patients may present requiring treatment who have a history of prostate cancer treated elsewhere with no confirmatory records immediately available. Other patients may have had prior bad experiences with an earlier biopsy and are now refusing all-new diagnostic procedures. Medical conditions such as a recent cardiac stent placement or a history of pulmonary embolisms requiring prolonged anticoagulant therapy may also preclude performing a biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostatic Urethral Lift -- Preparation -- Preoperative Workup
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic workup for PUL mandates urinalysis with microscopy, culture, and sensitivity to exclude infection or unexplained hematuria. PSA measurement is a crucial screening modality preceding BPH interventions to mitigate the risk of delayed prostate cancer diagnosis. Should PSA levels be elevated, malignancy must be ruled out before performing a PUL. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1517
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate and open prostatectomy are both classified under the broader category of عمليات البروستاتا (prostate procedures). As prostate procedures, they represent distinct interventions used in the evaluation and management of prostate disease: the transrectal biopsy of prostate is a diagnostic technique that obtains tissue samples for histologic assessment, while open prostatectomy is a surgical approach that removes prostate tissue to treat conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia or localized prostate cancer. Recognizing that both procedures belong to the same overarching group of prostate operations helps clinicians select appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic strategies within the context of prostate health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: the primary site of prostatic disorder and related male genital diseases. Common pathologies include benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد), prostatic enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي), and prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا, سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Therapeutic strategies comprise transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for BPH and related enlargement, open prostatectomy (which may involve partial removal of adjacent structures such as the seminal vesicles), and transurethral prostate incision. Urinary tract procedures (عملية في السبيل البولي) frequently employ an indwelling catheter as a parent entity, and systematic screening (التحصّي) of male genital disorders emphasizes early detection and monitoring of prostate and related conditions, illustrating the integrated anatomy, pathology, and treatment framework in male genitourinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Open prostatectomy is classified as an operation by approach, indicating that it is a surgical procedure performed through a direct, open incision to access the prostate. As a member of the broader category of operations by approach, open prostatectomy exemplifies surgeries that rely on an open technique rather than minimally invasive or endoscopic methods, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies and informing patients about the nature of the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Prostatolithotomy, prostatotomy with calculus removal, and transurethral prostate incision are all classified as “other or therapeutic procedures; male genital,” and each of these interventions is performed directly on the prostate. The prostate itself is identified as the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer and serves as the location for prostatic disorders. Other male genital disorders are likewise situated in the prostate, are considered a subset of prostatic disorders, and are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships highlight the prostate as a central focus for both disease processes—such as metastatic cancer and various prostatic disorders—and for therapeutic procedures aimed at treating conditions within this organ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2611
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is a specific diagnostic procedure performed on the male genital system that falls under the broader category of prostate operations (عمليات البروستاتا). As a child technique within the group of male genital diagnostic procedures, the transrectal approach is used to obtain tissue samples from the prostate for pathological evaluation, thereby linking it directly to the larger framework of prostate‑related surgical interventions. This relationship highlights how the transrectal biopsy serves both as a focused diagnostic tool and as part of the comprehensive set of procedures encompassed by prostate operations.
